votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the factorial and sum functions, and the specification accurately checks the length and the elements of the list according to the parity of their 1-based index, matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. factorial and sum_1_to_i compute the same values as their Coq counterparts. The f_spec function checks that the list has length n and that for each i from 1 to n, the (i-1)th element equals factorial(i) if i is even, or sum_1_to_i(i) if i is odd. Python's i % 2 == 0 matches Coq's Nat.even, and the list indexing l[i-1] corresponds to nth (i-1) l 0. The logic produces the same boolean/Prop result for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. factorial and sum_1_to_i compute the same values as their Coq counterparts. The f_spec function checks that the list has length n and that for each i from 1 to n, the element at index i-1 equals factorial(i) if i is even, or sum_1_to_i(i) if i is odd. This matches the Coq spec exactly: Nat.even checks if i is even (i % 2 == 0 in Python), and nth (i-1) l 0 accesses the same index as l[i-1]. The Coq default value 0 for out-of-bounds access doesn't matter since the length check ensures valid indices.
Both compute factorial and sum 1..i identically, enforce len(l)=n, and for i=1..n check l[i-1] equals factorial(i) if i is even else sum_1_to_i(i), matching the Coq spec.
